title: I keep coming back to telepresence rituals
summary: A personal essay about telepresence rituals, and the longer argument hidden inside how coordination can feel intimate and brutal at the same time.
topic: telepresence rituals when the infrastructure finally became visible
body: I wrote this down in August 2020 after thinking about telepresence rituals from the vantage point of the unnerving quiet of a masked grocery line. The better question, for me, was not the headline one but the one underneath it: what kind of life is this idea assuming? In these conversations, technology usually erases that part first. We keep prioritizing capability, performance, and scale while deferring the question of whether the system improves daily life. Telepresence rituals seemed to me like a good way to keep the older question in view.

What interested me most was not the obvious mechanism from the demo version. Public language likes the visible layer, the robot, the interface, the model, the thing that screenshots well. What makes most tools matter is less the demo than the secondary effects around it. They change pacing. They decide who can go on operating through judgment and who must become parseable. They make different things visible or ignorable because the workflow beneath the surface has shifted. Once you notice that shift, telepresence rituals stop looking like a gadget and start looking like a theory of everyday life with a power source attached.

I do not think atmosphere is a soft concern. It is where many technologies tell the truth about themselves. Some leave people feeling more capable and less managed. Others quietly convert daily life into a set of nudges, checks, and approvals. That effect may not show up in a benchmark chart, but it absolutely shows up in the lived experience of the system.

Looking back from August 2020, I could already feel my own position shifting. A few months earlier I had written “A note on public health dashboards after convenience turned into dependency,” and the difference between that piece and this one is instructive. The earlier piece stayed nearer the surface appeal than this one does. The applause had stopped being the interesting part. The interesting part was the burden that arrived after it. I do not think that earlier optimism was exactly wrong. It was incomplete in the familiar way optimism becomes incomplete before the machinery has touched enough lives.

I do not see withdrawal from technology as the right lesson here. The better goal is to become harder to flatter. Useful tools should invite scrutiny too, especially about the world they normalize. If that sounds like a modest ambition, good. Most of the sanest writing I know begins modestly. The sane version keeps the frame narrow until the world forces it wider. That is a lesson I hear again when I reread “On repair culture before venture language flattened the mood.”

A final note to myself from August 2020: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. The debate about the future holds me less than the pressure a tool puts on its nearest neighbors. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The sleek version is often how the essential part slips past us.

references.title: A note on public health dashboards after convenience turned into dependency
On repair culture before venture language flattened the mood
